1.headline: China's robots are learning to run
1.body: Over 300 humanoid robots just raced a half-marathon, proving that physical AI is rapidly leaving the controlled lab environment. For students, this is the first glimpse of how robotics might actually scale in the wild.

1.headline: What 300 robots reveal about AI
1.body:
> Real-world navigation is improving
> Hardware and software are aligning
> Demos are becoming practical benchmarks

1.headline: Physical AI just got a stress test
1.body: Moving 300 humanoids over a half-marathon distance exposes exactly where current robotics fails and where it thrives. These events aren't just spectacles — they are how we benchmark real-world deployment.

3.headline: Robots are leaving the test lab
3.body: China's robot race shows embodied AI is getting more stable in motion and navigation. The real signal for students: physical AI is moving from demos toward deployment.

3.headline: The race is about control now
3.body:
> Navigation got noticeably better
> Physical systems are catching up
> Real-world use still needs polish

3.headline: Embodied AI is getting less fake
3.body: These robots are not just faster than before — they are better at staying upright, following routes, and handling the messiness of the real world. That gap matters if robotics is your next field.

4.headline: Humanoid robots just finished a marathon
4.body: Hundreds of robots raced in China, showcasing serious progress in embodied AI stability and control. For robotics engineering, the lab-to-field gap is finally narrowing.

4.headline: How 300 robots are training for reality
4.body:
> Embodied intelligence is advancing fast
> Stability on uneven ground improves
> Deployment is still the challenge

4.headline: Robotics just moved past the demo stage
4.body: Watching 300 humanoids race proves that physical AI is no longer about static balance. It’s about movement in the wild — the exact problem current robotics teams are now solving.
